dialogue.utterance: Unit 2, respond to ER Bed 2 for interfacility transfer of chest pain patient.
Hello, I'm Mark, an EMT with [CITY] EMS. The call is for a transfer/interfacility/palliative care. I'll need you to describe what brought you to 911 today.
Her complaint is chest pain that started about two days ago.
Can you open your eyes for me, Ma'am?
Yes, I can.
What is your name?
Mary Johnson.
Can you tell me where you are right now, Ms. Johnson?
I'm in the ER at [COMPANY_NAME].
Would you tell me the current year?
If it's okay with you, I'd like you to squeeze my fingers and raise both of your arms.
Sure.
Your airway appears clear and unobstructed. I will now assess your breathing.
Your breathing appears even with equal chest rise and no signs of distress.
Partner, can you check her SpO2 and count her breathing?
SpO₂ is 99% using room air.
Her respiratory rate is 16, and you're breathing steadily with no signs of difficulty.
Your skin is pink, warm, and dry with no signs of major injury.
I feel a strong and regular pulse at 49 beats per minute.
I have felt your peripheral pulses, and they are present without swelling or edema.
Physical examination confirms a pulse of 49.
Her blood pressure is 143/84.
EKG result shows sinus bradycardia. I'll apply the 4-lead monitoring now.
Would you describe the nature of your chest pain?
It feels like pressure, mainly on the left side of my chest.
Does the pain radiate to any part of your body?
Yes, into my left jaw.
Does your chest pain change with any movement or body position?
It is worse when I press on my chest, but it is relieved when I lean forward.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how severe is your current pain?
It's about a 6.
How long has the pain been present, and were you doing anything when it began?
The pain has been for about two days without a clear trigger at the onset.
Could you list the medications you regularly take?
She is currently taking Lasix, ranolazine, albuterol, and a few others.
Would you describe any medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
I have osteoarthritis, asthma, CHF, lupus, hypertension, and a history of a stroke.
Have you experienced chest discomfort or heart-related issues before?
Yes, and the pain I experience now has not improved with nitroglycerin or Dilaudid previously.
Are you allergic to any medications or substances?
I don't know of any drug or food allergies.
You appear alert and oriented with no evidence of confusion or dizziness.
She is alert, oriented to person, place, time, and event (A&Ox4).
Her skin is pink, warm, and dry with no rashes or wounds noted.
Have you experienced any dizziness, weakness, or trouble remembering things?
No, I feel alert and stable.
We are following the chest pain - suspected cardiac protocol 2-1 for your condition.
I'll place another round of 4-lead ECG monitoring on you.
The EKG remains in sinus bradycardia with no new changes.
The pulse has dropped to 50.
Her SpO₂ level is now 98%.
Her respiratory rate is still 16 breaths per minute.
Her blood pressure has slightly increased to 167/82.
We do not need to check glucose since an IV line is already in place and there are no symptoms of hypoglycemia.
No abnormal facial droop, arm drift, or coordination has been observed.
She also shows no sign of confusion or slurred speech-her mental status is intact.
You're being transferred to [COMPANY_NAME] for a scheduled cardiac catheterization.
We are now ready to help you onto the stretcher.
I'll stay close by to support her during the move.
She is now seated and secured on the stretcher.
I'll inform the receiving hospital prior to arrival about her current vitals and condition.
Upon arrival, I will give a face-to-face report to their ER team.
She is clean and ready for transport.
All our assessments are complete, and she's secure. Let's begin the transport.
4-lead monitoring is in place and stable at this time.
During assessment, she had no signs of altered mental status or AMS.
No signs of obstruction were found despite my remarks-your airway is visually unobstructed.
She remained alert through the full assessment with no interruption in responsiveness.
No evidence of major bleeding was noted during the primary survey or skin examination.
Her SpO₂ remains stable, with only minor changes observed.
We'll keep you on monitoring until arrival at the hospital.
I repeated the skin inspection and found no new rashes, bruises, or signs of trauma.
The EKG still reflects sinus bradycardia without any additional findings.
Has there been any pain you've experienced recently that doesn't seem related to your chest discomfort?
No, the chest pain I've had for two days is the only pain I'm experiencing.
When did the pain first start concerning you enough to seek help?
I haven't needed care yet, but the ER staff knows about the pain lasting two days.
Do you have a history of taking nitroglycerin or dilaudid, and how has it worked for you in the past?
Yes, but neither provided relief this time.
Her IV remains intact and secured for transport.
The patient has been verified to have no known drug or food allergies.
The troponin was negative and no STEMI was found, so her symptoms may be non-cardiac at this point.
She is not requiring airway maneuvers or additional oxygen at this time.
No lymphadenopathy or neck masses were observed.
I heard no abnormal breath sounds or heart murmurs.
No tenderness or masses observed during the abdominal inspection.
No active bleeding or open wounds were present during the transport setup.
She has no signs of jaundice or rash, and her turgor remains normal.
We are heading to [COMPANY_NAME] immediately for their scheduled cardiac cath in the morning.
She stayed alert to voice the entire time with no signs of regression.
No deformity noted on her arms or legs and pulses are still present bilaterally.
Blood pressure remains stable during the reassessments; no hemodynamic instability.
Care will be transferred at bed 35 using the EMS stretcher by placing her from the stretcher to the hospital bed.
Prior to transport, she signed a consent form for treatment, transport, and billing.
The patient is secured x5 on the stretcher and ready for transport.
I will call ahead to confirm your arrival and ensure a smooth handover to the ER team.
Emergency services will regroup at the station after the transfer.
I will give a verbatim in-person report once we arrive.
All transport items are in place and status notes have been transferred to the receiving hospital.
I will continue to follow the guideline for transfers with chest pain, monitoring for any changes.
I checked her mouth and throat and found no obstructions or drainage.
How has your condition changed over these two days?
There hasn't been much change-it remains steady with no worsening signs.
She remains sinus bradycardiac with consistent pulse throughout the transport.
If needed, we are set up with the EKG machine and ready to respond to changes at any point.
She is still A&Ox4 with normal extraocular movements and no rigidity in her neck.
I will ensure the wheel is locked during the loading sequence.
Her oxygen saturation is consistent with mild ambient room air and does not require supplemental oxygen.
We've stabilized her and will transport safely to avoid additional discomfort.
I've reviewed all our procedures and tools and all are fully functional.
Upon arrival, the ER will know the patient's medical history, current vital signs, and planned next steps.
